name: Adnan (Adnanite lineage)
ancestorStory: Descended from Ishmael, son of Abraham
description: Northern Arabs / Arabized Arabs / al-Arab al-Mustarabah. Origin: Hejaz, Central/North Arabia.

subTribes.name: Mudar
subTribes.legitimacyNotes: Modern scholarship treats these genealogies with caution, noting that they reflect later attempts to systematize tribal relations and political rivalries rather than straightforward history. The confederation included diverse tribes with varying lifestyles and political orientations grouped together under the collective name 'Mudar'.
subTribes.ancestorName: Mudar b. Nizār b. Maʿadd b. ʿAdnān
subTribes.ancestorStory: Genealogists trace Mudar's descent from Mudar b. Nizār b. Maʿadd b. ʿAdnān, placing him among the foundational ancestors of the so-called 'northern Arabs'. Mudar is said to have had two sons, from whom the major branches of the confederation descended. Genealogical narratives also preserve legendary elements, such as the division of Nizār's inheritance and symbolic associations.
subTribes.traditionalEconomy: Mixed - ranging from sedentary groups associated with sanctuary towns to highly mobile pastoral nomads
subTribes.description: Mudar was one of the principal tribal confederations of northern and central Arabia in late antiquity and the early Islamic period. Rising to power over the Hejaz in the fifth century after supplanting the Banu Ghassan, Mudar occupied large areas of the Hejaz, Tihamah, Najd, and adjoining regions. Together with Rabīʿa, it formed one of the two largest groupings of tribes tracing descent from Nizar ibn Maʿadd. In later historiography, 'Mudar' came to designate not only a genealogical lineage but also a broad political and cultural bloc opposed to the southern (Yemeni) tribal groupings.

subTribes.name: Rabi'ah
subTribes.legitimacyNotes: Established blood lineage recognized by classical Arab genealogists as one of the two main Adnanite branches
subTribes.ancestorStory: Rabi'a ibn Nizar is considered the patriarch of one of the two main branches of the North Arabian (Adnanite) tribes, representing a major division in Arabian tribal genealogy.
subTribes.traditionalEconomy: Mixed - bedouin pastoralism and sedentary agriculture, with some branches being mostly sedentary while others remained nomadic
subTribes.description: Rabi'a ibn Nizar is the patriarch of one of two main branches of the 'North Arabian' (Adnanite) tribes, the other being founded by Mudhar. The tribe has numerous important branches including Abd al-Qays, Anizah, Banu Bakr, Taghlib, and others. Originally from the Tihamah region of western Arabia, they migrated northwards and eastwards, with different branches settling across the Arabian Peninsula, Mesopotamia, and eventually Egypt. Many were Christian before Islam, and the tribe produced numerous notable figures throughout Islamic history.

subTribes.name: Qays Aylan
subTribes.description: Key Adnanite branch

subTribes.name: Anizzah
subTribes.legitimacyNotes: Well-documented blood lineage within the established Rabiʿa-Adnanite genealogical framework, with historical continuity from pre-Islamic times through modern era.
subTribes.ancestorStory: The classical Arab genealogists placed Anizah within the large Rabiʿa branch of the Adnanites alongside tribes of Abd al-Qays, Banu Bakr, Bani Hanifa, and Taghlib. Anizah's eponymous ancestor is described as a great uncle of all these related tribes, indicating they come from the same family and most likely one patriarch.
subTribes.traditionalEconomy: Mixed economy of pastoral nomadism and tribute collection. Nomadic branches practiced seasonal migration between northern Arabian steppes and Syria, while levying crops from oasis inhabitants and collecting khuwwa (brotherhood tribute) from Syrian farmers in exchange for protection.
subTribes.description: Anizah (Arabic: عنزة) is a major Arabian tribe with presence across the Arabian Peninsula, Upper Mesopotamia, and the Levant. Their existence as an autonomous tribal group predates Islam, and they are classified within the Rabiʿa branch of the Adnanites. The tribe has both nomadic and sedentary branches, with the nomadic branch living in northern Arabian steppes and the sedentary branch in Al-Yamama district of eastern Najd. They became prominent masters of oasis towns like Khaybar and Al-'Ula during the Ottoman era, collecting tribute from agricultural communities while maintaining seasonal migration patterns.

subTribes.name: Bani Tamim
subTribes.legitimacyNotes: Claims direct Adnanite lineage through a well-documented genealogical tradition tracing back to Abraham through Ishmael. This appears to be a genuine blood lineage claim rather than a political confederation.
subTribes.ancestorStory: According to the traditional family tree, Tamim ibn Murr ibn 'Udd ibn Amr ibn Ilyas ibn Mudar bin Nizar bin Ma'add bin Adnan - claimed to be a direct descendant of Isma'il bin Ibrahim (Ishmael, son of Abraham).
subTribes.peakPowerEra: Early Islamic period through Abbasid era
subTribes.description: The Banū Tamīm are one of the largest Arab tribes that originated in Najd and Hejaz in the Arabian Peninsula. They are an Adnanite tribe with a claimed direct lineage to Abraham through Ishmael. The tribe occupied numerous Wadis and villages in central and eastern Arabia in the 6th century and played an important role in the beginning of Islam. They came into contact with Muhammad in the 8th year of Hijrah but did not immediately convert to Islam. The word Tamim in Arabic means strong and solid, and can also mean those who strive for perfection.

subTribes.name: Bani Yas
subTribes.legitimacyNotes: This is a genuine tribal confederation that formed through political alliance and shared settlement patterns, with the Al Bu Falah clan holding the most power. The confederation structure allowed for integration of associated tribes under Abu Dhabi's authority while maintaining some autonomous characteristics.
subTribes.ancestorStory: The Bani Yas tribe originated in the Najd region of Central Arabia, descending, along with the Awamir, from Yas ibn Amer ibn Sa'sa'a.
subTribes.description: The Bani Yas is a tribal confederation of Najdi origin in the United Arab Emirates, consisting of around 20 tribes from Abu Dhabi to Dubai to Khor Al Adaid in southeast Qatar. As the largest single tribe among the indigenous tribal population of what is now the UAE, they formed a powerful confederation called the Bani Yas Coalition. The Al Nahyan (Al Bu Falah branch) leads the tribe and rules Abu Dhabi, while the Al Maktoum (Al Bu Falasah branch) rules Dubai, making them the most influential families in UAE federal government.

subTribes.name: Otaibah
subTribes.legitimacyNotes: The tribe claims descent from pre-Islamic Hawazin through documented genealogical records. While most genealogy is based on oral tradition dating to the Middle Ages and earlier, there is scholarly verification through historical manuscripts including Hisham ibn al-Kalbi's 8th-century work 'The Great Ancestry'.
subTribes.ancestorName: Otaibah ibn Guzayah ibn Jusham ibn Sa'd ibn Bakr ibn Hawazin
subTribes.ancestorStory: According to the 8th-century manuscript by Hisham ibn al-Kalbi, 'Otaibah' is attributed to a standard or banner belonging to the tribe of Hawazin. The name derives from Otaibah Ibn Guzayah Ibn Jusham Ibn Sa'd ibn Bakr Ibn Hawazin. After most Hawazin clans departed from Hejaz and Najd, those who remained formed the largest Hawazin alliance and were named Otaibah.
subTribes.description: The Otaibah is one of the biggest Arabian tribes originating in the Arabian Peninsula, with distribution spanning throughout Saudi Arabia, especially in Najd and Hejaz, and the Middle East. They are descended from the Bedouin and trace back to the Mudar family, belonging to the Qays ʿAylān confederacy through its previous name, Hawazin. The tribe remained as the largest Hawazin alliance after most other Hawazin clans departed from Hejaz and Najd to other lands including the Levant, Iraq, Egypt, North Africa, and Persia.

subTribes.name: Harb
subTribes.legitimacyNotes: The text only briefly mentions 'Harb tribe in the Arabian peninsula' without providing details about its formation, lineage claims, or historical context.
subTribes.ancestorStory: <UNKNOWN>
subTribes.description: Harb is an Arabian Peninsula tribe mentioned in the research text, though specific details about the tribe itself are not provided in the source material.

subTribes.name: Mutayr
subTribes.legitimacyNotes: The tribal lineage appears to be authentic blood lineage supported by DNA testing showing shared haplogroups and common ancestry among members. The tribe has embraced genetic genealogy to study their genealogy using modern technologies.
subTribes.ancestorStory: According to Ahmad al-Qalqashandi (d. 1418), the Mutayr tribe belongs to Ghatafan who are descendants of Ishmael son of Abraham. DNA tests have confirmed that male participants from Mutayr share the same haplogroup and common ancestor, with most Y-Chromosome lineage from the J1 Haplogroup network.
subTribes.description: Mutayr is a large Arab tribe originating from the northern Hejaz near Medina, whose territory extends from the Hijaz to Najd and then to Kuwait. The tribe underwent a major eastward migration in the 17th century into central Arabia, becoming the dominant nomadic tribe of Al-Qaseem. They played a crucial role in early 20th century Arabian history through their involvement in the Ikhwan movement under Faisal Al-Dewish. Originally Bedouin, the tribe has now largely settled in cities and towns across Saudi Arabia, establishing up to 400 villages across the country.

subTribes.name: Banu Khalid
subTribes.legitimacyNotes: Appears to be a genuine tribal lineage with established chieftainship structure through the Al Humaid clan
subTribes.traditionalEconomy: desert nomadism and control of trade routes
subTribes.description: The Bani Khalid were a powerful Arabian tribe that dominated eastern Arabia from the 17th to 19th centuries. They established the Bani Khalid Emirate in 1669 under Emir Barrak ibn Ghurayr, controlling the Al-Hasa and Al-Qatif regions after expelling Ottoman forces. The tribe's chieftainship was traditionally held by the Al Humaid clan. Known for their military prowess, they were staunch enemies of the emerging Saudi state and the Wahhabi movement. Their power declined after repeated defeats by Saudi-tribal alliances, with their rule ending definitively in 1830, though they briefly regained some authority under Ottoman patronage in 1874.

subTribes.name: Subay'
subTribes.legitimacyNotes: Established blood lineage tracing to the ancient and powerful Banu 'Amir tribe that dominated central Arabia in medieval times
subTribes.ancestorStory: The family traces its lineage to the large, ancient tribe of Banu 'Amir, also known as 'Amir ibn Sa'sa'a, who came to dominate central Arabia in the medieval period.
subTribes.description: Subaie' is an Arabian tribe living in the center of southern Najd. The tribe comprises both nomadic (bedouin) and sedentary sections, with the sedentary members having resided in central Arabia for centuries. Families belonging to Subay' can be found in practically every town in the region, making up a disproportionate amount of central Arabia's traditional sedentary population. They were known as Prophet Mohammed's knights and have served as knights for the first and second Saudi states as well as the current Kingdom of Saudi Arabia.

subTribes.name: Banu Hanifa
subTribes.legitimacyNotes: Classical Arab genealogists counted them as a Christian branch of Bani Bakr, but they led an independent existence prior to Islam. Later assimilated into Anizah tribe.
subTribes.description: Banu Hanifa is an ancient Arab tribe that inhabited the area of al-Yamama in central Saudi Arabia. Members of the great Rabi'ah branch of North Arabian tribes, they were primarily sedentary farmers living in settlements along the wadis of eastern Nejd. The tribe played a significant role in early Islamic history, initially converting to Islam but then apostatizing under their own prophet Musaylima, leading to the Ridda Wars. After their defeat, they rejoined the Islamic state and later spread to various regions including Iraq and Egypt. The ruling House of Saud of Saudi Arabia are Hanifites, and according to King Salman Al Saud, the tribe eventually assimilated into the larger Anizah tribe.

name: Ajman (tribe)
legitimacyNotes: Established tribal confederation with clear lineage to Banu Yam and Qahtanite roots, though now mostly settled rather than nomadic
ancestorStory: Al-Ajman is a Qahtanite Arab tribe that is descended from Banu Yam tribe.
description: Al-Ajman or al-'Ijman is an Arabian tribal confederation in the Arabian Peninsula, with Ajman spread across Saudi Arabia, Qatar, the United Arab Emirates and Kuwait. The Ajman were noted for being important players in the politics of eastern Arabia in the 18th and early 20th centuries. Nearly all the Ajman have abandoned nomadic life and have settled in the Persian Gulf states, particularly in the Eastern Province of Saudi Arabia.

name: Al Bu Falah
legitimacyNotes: Originally from Bani Malik lineage but transformed into a confederation through warfare alliance against Ottoman influence. Various sections claim different Arab tribal origins but united under Albu Salih identity.
traditionalEconomy: agriculture in marshland areas, pastoral nomadism, and trade
description: The Albu Salih is an Arab tribe from the Bani Malik of the Al-Muntafiq confederation. During the 18th century, they transformed from a traditional tribe into a powerful tribal confederation (warfare alliance) in opposition to Ottoman Empire influence. Under the leadership of Shaikh Badr Al Rumaiyadh, they became the most influential tribe in the Bani Malik region of the Muntafiq Emirate. The tribe historically consisted of both settled villagers near the great marshes and semi-nomadic Bedouins who migrated seasonally between their territory in southern Iraq and the Arabian Gulf coast. They were known to be well-armed and engaged in trade during their migrations. By the 1940s-1950s, most nomadic members had settled permanently.

description: The Albuflasa (Al Bu Falasah) is described as a Bedouin clan with members in Bahrain. The tribe appears to be integrated into modern Bahraini society, with members serving in various professional capacities including the military and civil service.

name: Al Bu Hamir
legitimacyNotes: A genuine subsection of the larger Bani Yas confederation, historically recognized as part of the Manasir tribal structure
ancestorStory: <UNKNOWN>
description: The Al Bu Hamir is a small section of the Bani Yas tribal confederation, originating in Al Ain Region of the emirate of Abu Dhabi. They were considered a subsection of the Manasir under the Bani Yas structure. The tribe comprised approximately 60 houses in Abu Dhabi around the turn of the 20th century, where they appear to have settled. Members of the tribe have traditionally enjoyed close relationships with the Al Nahyan ruling family, with several holding important positions in Sheikh Zayed Bin Sultan Al Nahyan's administration and Royal court. They were Maliki Sunni in religion and partisans of the Hinawi faction in politics.

description: The Al Bu Muhair (singular Al Muhairi or Al Mheiri or Al Mehairi) are a tribe of the United Arab Emirates (UAE), closely associated with the Bani Yas of Abu Dhabi but settled throughout the western coastal areas of the Emirates. At the turn of the 20th century, they were mostly settled with a minority of some 20 households in Abu Dhabi still following a Bedouin lifestyle. The tribe has been distributed across various Emirates including Dubai, Sharjah, Ajman, Umm Al Quwain, and Ras Al Khaimah.

ancestorStory: <UNKNOWN>
traditionalEconomy: Bedouin nomadism, later settled agriculture in oases and strategic coastal settlements
description: The Al Bu Shamis is an Arab tribe of the United Arab Emirates, also evident in northern Oman. Originally one of three sections of the Na'im confederation (along with Al Bu Kharaiban and Khawatir), the Al Bu Shamis has become virtually independent and closely associated with the ruling Al Bu Falah of Abu Dhabi. Originally forming the Bedouin component of the Na'im, they retained a predominantly nomadic character into the 1950s with approximately 1,000 individuals including around 400 armed men. They later settled strategically significant oases and coastal settlements, becoming headmen in places like Hamriyah and Al Heera.

name: Al Murrah
description: The Al Murrah are an Arab tribe descended from the Banu Yam tribe who historically were camel-herding nomads controlling and travelling through vast areas of the Arabian Peninsula. They comprise approximately 15,000 individuals with seven clans making up the Saudi branch, and historically made up a large proportion of Qatar's ethnic population. The tribe has been involved in significant political events in Qatar, including supporting former Emir Sheikh Khalifa Al Thani and later attempting a failed coup against Sheikh Hamad Al Thani in 1996.

description: The Al Nuaimi is the ruling royal family of Ajman, one of the seven emirates of the UAE. They are part of the Na'im tribal confederation, specifically from the Al Bu Kharaiban section. They established control over Ajman in 1816-1817 and have ruled continuously since then. The Al Nuaimi also have historical presence in Al Buraimi oasis in Oman, where they were original inhabitants and rulers.

traditionalEconomy: nomadic pastoralism and later urban settlement
description: Al-Awazem is a large tribal confederation in the Arabian Peninsula, Syria, Lebanon, Jordan and Egypt, with the majority of its current settled population residing in Kuwait, northeastern Saudi Arabia, and Bahrain. They are among the largest tribes in Kuwait and were historically known for their military prowess, having fought the Ottomans, Achaemenids (Iranians), and Bani Rasheed. They once fought seven tribes to protect the Emirate of Diriyah.

legitimacyNotes: Multiple historical sources confirm their lineage from Banu Lam through Tayy, making this a well-documented blood lineage rather than a political formation.
ancestorStory: The tribe genealogically came from Banu Lam and descended from Tayy. According to historical sources including Muhammad Amin al-Baghdadi, Hamad al-Jassir, and Ibn Fadlallah al-Umari, they came from a place opposite Medina named al-Dhaghn.
description: Al-Dhafeer is a Bedouin Arab tribe that descends from Tayy through Banu Lam. Known historically as the 'People of flowing hair and camel caravans' due to their habit of raiding alongside women and children and their enthusiasm for victories. The tribe spread throughout Arabia through raiding and migration without proper settlement until 1926 when Ibn Saud allowed their Saudi branch to settle in several settlements after securing roads. They originated from areas near Medina and migrated through central Najd to northern Najd, northern Bahrain, and the deserts of southern Iraq and northern Kuwait.

subTribes.name: Bani Utbah
subTribes.legitimacyNotes: Political confederation of multiple clans that migrated together from Najd. Most clans claim Anizah lineage with some exceptions. Formation was based on shared migration and political cooperation rather than pure blood lineage.
subTribes.description: The Bani Utbah is an Arab tribal confederation in the Arabian Peninsula, formed when a group of clans from Najd migrated to Eastern Arabia in the late 17th and early 18th centuries. Most Utub clans trace their lineage to the Anizah tribe, with some exceptions like Al Bin Ali tracing to Banu Sulaym. The confederation includes prominent ruling families like Al-Sabah and Al-Khalifa, and successfully conquered Bahrain in 1783, establishing lasting rule there.

name: Awamir
legitimacyNotes: A large tribe that originated in the steppes to the north of the Hadhramut and settled in the north through a migration process over 500 years. Appears to be a genuine tribal lineage rather than a political confederation.
ancestorStory: <UNKNOWN>
description: The Awamir is a Bedouin Arab tribe in the United Arab Emirates and Oman, closely associated with the Bani Yas of Abu Dhabi. Warlike and fiercely independent, they were traditionally camel breeders, raiders and occasionally date farmers before settling in the 1960s. They roamed across the whole Oman peninsula, from Muscat and Nizwa to Abu Dhabi and Liwa, and were characterized as reputed brave and warlike but also crafty, treacherous and predatory.

relations.context: Desert nomads allied with Bani Yas in Abu Dhabi

The Awamir in the area of Abu Dhabi were considered affiliated to the Bani Yas and frequently supported them in conflicts. They formed part of tribal confederacy forces and supported various Al Nahyan rulers in military campaigns.
By the 1920s, the Awamir fought alongside the Manasir, Duru and Bani Qitab in conflicts around Buraimi and Abu Dhabi town.
The Awamir had long been in conflict with the Duru, and in the 1940s this ongoing conflict led the headman of the Bedouin Awamir to lead his people to Al Hasa in 1943.

description: The Azd (Arabic: أَزْد), or Al-Azd (Arabic: ٱلْأَزْد), is an ancient Arabian tribe originating from Northern Arabia. The lands of Azd occupied an area west of Bisha and Al Bahah in what is today Saudi Arabia. The Azd tribal group originally lived in Yemen according to medieval folklore, until the collapse of the Ma'rib Dam in the late 3rd century CE when they began emigrating to other parts of the Arabian Peninsula.

subTribes.name: Zahran
subTribes.legitimacyNotes: This appears to be a genuine blood lineage tracing back to the ancient Azd confederation, with well-documented genealogical records and historical presence in the Arabian Peninsula.
subTribes.ancestorName: Zahran ibn Ka'ab ibn Al-Harith ibn Ka'ab ibn Abdullah Ibn Mālik ibn Nasr ibn Al-Azd
subTribes.ancestorStory: Zahran is the name of the shared common ancestor of the tribe. Etymological sources indicate it is of Arabic Semitic origin, meaning 'bright' and 'pure'. The full lineage traces back to Al-Azd through Zahran ibn Ka'ab.
subTribes.description: Zahran (Arabic: زهران), also known as Banū ʿZahrān ibn Kaʿab, is one of the oldest Arabian tribes in the Arabian Peninsula and is regarded as one of the largest tribes in Al Bahah Province. Al Baha is the homeland of Zahran and Ghamid. The tribe is well-known from pre-Islamic times and many joined Prophet Muhammad in Medina during the early Islamic period. The name Zahran means 'bright' and 'pure' in Arabic.

subTribes.name: Ghamid
subTribes.legitimacyNotes: Documented blood lineage tracing back through Azd Shanu'ah to Qahtan, with clear genealogical records and tribal presence maintained from pre-Islamic times to present
subTribes.ancestorStory: The Ghamid tribe traces its lineage back to their forefather Ghamid, who is Amr ibn Ka'b — and some say Amr ibn Abdullah ibn Ka'b — ibn Al-Harith ibn Ka'b ibn Abdullah ibn Malik ibn Nasr, who is Shanu'ah of the Azd. There are different etymological explanations for the name, with Abu Hatim al-Sijistani and al-Asma'i stating it derives from 'Ghamadtu al-bi'r Ghamdan' meaning abundant water.
subTribes.traditionalEconomy: Agriculture and trade (benefited from Arabia Felix region with more rainfall and productive fields)
subTribes.description: The Ghamd (also transliterated as Ghamid) is an Azd Arab tribe of the Hejaz Region, predominantly Sunni, and considered one of the oldest tribes of the Arabian Peninsula. The Ghamid people are thought to be closely related to the neighboring tribe of Zahran. They have maintained their presence in their historical homeland from pre-Islamic times to the present day, and were significantly involved in early Islamic expansion and various historical conflicts.

They are one of the two main sections of the Shihuh tribe, along with the Bani Shatair. The tribe is divided into sections including the Kumzarah and Habus. subTribes.description: The Habus is an Arab tribe primarily settled in Ras Al Khaimah, UAE, around Khatt, Fahlain and the Hajar Mountain wadis. They are an agrarian mountain people who cultivated extensive farmland in the wadis of the Hajar Mountains, particularly Wadi Naqab and Wadi Bih. The tribe is known for speaking the distinctive Shehhi dialect of Arabic with Himyarite Yemeni origins dating to the second century BCE. Fiercely independent, they successfully resisted taxation attempts and maintained semi-autonomous status well into the 1960s. Their name is derived from their original holdings on Jebel Hibs mountain.

Originally from Abida near Sarat Abida (also known as 'alhirja' near Thahran Al Janoub), they are known for their trade activities and bringing foreign goods to Arabia. They migrated to Lower Najd in the early 13th century under Sheikh Mohammed Bin Shaba'an, then later to Al Ahsa'a. They are considered nomadic Bedouins, though many have settled in cities and hold government offices. They were honored by King Abdulaziz Al Saud with the title 'Khaznat Thafar' (Vault of Victory) for their crucial role in the Battle of Kinzan against Al Ajman in 1914-1915.

They came into contact with Muhammad in the 8th year of Hijrah but did not immediately convert to Islam. The word Tamim in Arabic means strong and solid, and can also mean those who strive for perfection.

Most Utub clans trace their lineage to the Anizah tribe, with some exceptions like Al Bin Ali tracing to Banu Sulaym. The confederation includes prominent ruling families like Al-Sabah and Al-Khalifa, and successfully conquered Bahrain in 1783, establishing lasting rule there.

ancestorStory: The Bani Yas tribe originated in the Najd region of Central Arabia, descending, along with the Awamir, from Yas ibn Amer ibn Sa'sa'a.
description: The Bani Yas is a tribal confederation of Najdi origin in the United Arab Emirates, consisting of around 20 tribes from Abu Dhabi to Dubai to Khor Al Adaid in southeast Qatar. As the largest single tribe among the indigenous tribal population of what is now the UAE, they formed a powerful confederation called the Bani Yas Coalition. The Al Nahyan (Al Bu Falah branch) leads the tribe and rules Abu Dhabi, while the Al Maktoum (Al Bu Falasah branch) rules Dubai, making them the most influential families in UAE federal government.

subTribes.name: Mazrui
subTribes.description: The Mazrui or Mazar'i were an Omani Arab clan that reigned over some areas of East Africa, especially Kenya, from the 18th to the 20th century. In the 18th century they governed Mombasa and other coastal places and opposed the Omani Al Bu Sa'id Dynasty that ruled over Zanzibar. They were one of the groups that most actively resisted British rule when the British East Africa Protectorate was established in the late 19th century, along with the Kikuyu and Kamba people.

The Al Qubaisi family shares close relations with the leading royal family of the United Arab Emirates through Salama bint Butti Al Qubaisi, mother of Sheikh Zayed bin Sultan Al Nahyan, the founder of the UAE. As a subset of the Bani Yas tribe, the Al Qubaisi family has been influential in various aspects of Emirati society, including politics, business, and community affairs. They have played roles in the development and progress of the UAE, contributing to various sectors and institutions within the country.

subTribes.description: The Albu Salih is an Arab tribe from the Bani Malik of the Al-Muntafiq confederation. During the 18th century, they transformed from a traditional tribe into a powerful tribal confederation (warfare alliance) in opposition to Ottoman Empire influence. Under the leadership of Shaikh Badr Al Rumaiyadh, they became the most influential tribe in the Bani Malik region of the Muntafiq Emirate. The tribe historically consisted of both settled villagers near the great marshes and semi-nomadic Bedouins who migrated seasonally between their territory in southern Iraq and the Arabian Gulf coast. They were known to be well-armed and engaged in trade during their migrations. By the 1940s-1950s, most nomadic members had settled permanently.

name: Banu Hanifa
legitimacyNotes: Classical Arab genealogists counted them as a Christian branch of Bani Bakr, but they led an independent existence prior to Islam. Later assimilated into Anizah tribe.
description: Banu Hanifa is an ancient Arab tribe that inhabited the area of al-Yamama in central Saudi Arabia. Members of the great Rabi'ah branch of North Arabian tribes, they were primarily sedentary farmers living in settlements along the wadis of eastern Nejd. The tribe played a significant role in early Islamic history, initially converting to Islam but then apostatizing under their own prophet Musaylima, leading to the Ridda Wars. After their defeat, they rejoined the Islamic state and later spread to various regions including Iraq and Egypt. The ruling House of Saud of Saudi Arabia are Hanifites, and according to King Salman Al Saud, the tribe eventually assimilated into the larger Anizah tribe.

subTribes.name: Anizzah
subTribes.legitimacyNotes: Well-documented blood lineage within the established Rabiʿa-Adnanite genealogical framework, with historical continuity from pre-Islamic times through modern era.
subTribes.ancestorStory: The classical Arab genealogists placed Anizah within the large Rabiʿa branch of the Adnanites alongside tribes of Abd al-Qays, Banu Bakr, Bani Hanifa, and Taghlib. Anizah's eponymous ancestor is described as a great uncle of all these related tribes, indicating they come from the same family and most likely one patriarch.
subTribes.traditionalEconomy: Mixed economy of pastoral nomadism and tribute collection. Nomadic branches practiced seasonal migration between northern Arabian steppes and Syria, while levying crops from oasis inhabitants and collecting khuwwa (brotherhood tribute) from Syrian farmers in exchange for protection.
subTribes.description: Anizah (Arabic: عنزة) is a major Arabian tribe with presence across the Arabian Peninsula, Upper Mesopotamia, and the Levant. Their existence as an autonomous tribal group predates Islam, and they are classified within the Rabiʿa branch of the Adnanites. The tribe has both nomadic and sedentary branches, with the nomadic branch living in northern Arabian steppes and the sedentary branch in Al-Yamama district of eastern Najd. They became prominent masters of oasis towns like Khaybar and Al-'Ula during the Ottoman era, collecting tribute from agricultural communities while maintaining seasonal migration patterns.

description: The Banu Hilal is a major Arab tribe from Najd in central Arabian Peninsula that migrated to the Maghreb in the 11th century, playing a crucial role in the Arabization of North Africa. They originated as nomadic Bedouins who ruled parts of Najd and campaigned in Iraq-Syria borderlands. The Fatimid Caliphate used them to punish the rebellious Zirids by dispatching them westward around 1050. Their migration, numbering between 150,000-300,000 people, fundamentally transformed the linguistic and cultural landscape of the Maghreb, spreading Bedouin Arabic and nomadic lifestyle across previously agricultural regions. They were organized into six main sub-tribes and became one of the most influential Arab tribal confederations in North African history.

description: The Bani Khalid were a powerful Arabian tribe that dominated eastern Arabia from the 17th to 19th centuries. They established the Bani Khalid Emirate in 1669 under Emir Barrak ibn Ghurayr, controlling the Al-Hasa and Al-Qatif regions after expelling Ottoman forces. The tribe's chieftainship was traditionally held by the Al Humaid clan. Known for their military prowess, they were staunch enemies of the emerging Saudi state and the Wahhabi movement. Their power declined after repeated defeats by Saudi-tribal alliances, with their rule ending definitively in 1830, though they briefly regained some authority under Ottoman patronage in 1874.

description: Himyar was a powerful ancient kingdom and polity in the southern highlands of Yemen that existed from 110 BCE until the early 6th century CE. Initially integrated into the Qatabanian kingdom until 110 BCE, it became an independent kingdom ruled by kings from the dhū-Raydān tribe. The kingdom was a confederation of tribes that created a wealthy, sophisticated society with rich trade networks linking the Mediterranean, Middle East, India, and East Africa. Himyar conquered neighboring kingdoms including Saba', Qataban, and Haḍramaut, uniting southern Arabia for the first time by 300 CE. The kingdom underwent significant religious transformations, converting from South Arabian polytheism to Judaism in the 4th century under Malkikarib Yuhamin, and later to Christianity after the Aksumite conquest in 530 CE.

subTribes.description: The Azd (Arabic: أَزْد), or Al-Azd (Arabic: ٱلْأَزْد), is an ancient Arabian tribe originating from Northern Arabia. The lands of Azd occupied an area west of Bisha and Al Bahah in what is today Saudi Arabia. The Azd tribal group originally lived in Yemen according to medieval folklore, until the collapse of the Ma'rib Dam in the late 3rd century CE when they began emigrating to other parts of the Arabian Peninsula.

subTribes.name: Tayy
subTribes.legitimacyNotes: Real blood lineage traced to Qahtanite origins, though Ibn Khaldun noted that Tayy intermarried with non-Arabs and did not preserve lineage purity strictly
subTribes.ancestorName: Julhumah ibn Udad (known as Tayy or Tayyi)
subTribes.ancestorStory: According to early Arab genealogists, Julhumah ibn Udad was the progenitor of the Tayy. His surname 'Ṭayyiʾ' supposedly derived from the Arabic word 'ṭawā' meaning 'to plaster' because he was said to be the first to have plastered the walls of a well. His ancestry was traced to Kahlan ibn Saba ibn Ya'rub, great-grandson of Qahtan.
subTribes.traditionalEconomy: pastoralism and control of trade routes
subTribes.description: The Tayy are a large and ancient Arab tribe, among whose descendants today are the tribes of Bani Sakher and Shammar. Originally based in Yemen, they migrated to northern Arabia in the late 2nd century CE and settled in the Shammar and Salma Mountains, which became known as Jabal Tayy. They established relations with both the Sasanian and Byzantine empires, supplanted the Lakhmids as rulers of al-Hirah in the 610s, and played significant roles throughout Islamic history. The tribe was split during the Fasad War, with the Jadila branch converting to Christianity and migrating to Syria, while the Ghawth branch remained in Jabal Tayy.

subTribes.name: Al Murrah
subTribes.description: The Al Murrah are an Arab tribe descended from the Banu Yam tribe who historically were camel-herding nomads controlling and travelling through vast areas of the Arabian Peninsula. They comprise approximately 15,000 individuals with seven clans making up the Saudi branch, and historically made up a large proportion of Qatar's ethnic population. The tribe has been involved in significant political events in Qatar, including supporting former Emir Sheikh Khalifa Al Thani and later attempting a failed coup against Sheikh Hamad Al Thani in 1996.

subTribes.name: Shammar
subTribes.legitimacyNotes: A genuine tribal confederation with three main branches (Abdah, Al-Aslam, and Sinjarah) that evolved from the larger Tayy tribal group. The name change from Tayy to Shammar occurred by the 14th century when they became known in literature as Shammar.
subTribes.ancestorStory: Descended from the Tayy tribe which migrated from Yemen to northern Arabian Peninsula. Led by Usma bin Luai, the Tayy invaded the mountains of Ajā and Salma from Banu Assad and Banu Tamim in northern Arabia in their exodus from Yemen in 115 CE.
subTribes.traditionalEconomy: nomadic camel-herding, horse-breeding, city-dwelling in Ha'il, and agriculture in oases
subTribes.description: The Shammar is a tribal Arab Qahtanite confederation descended from the Tayy, which migrated into northern Arabian Peninsula from Yemen in the second century. It is the largest branch of the Tayy and one of the largest and most influential Arab tribes. The historical seat of tribal leadership is in Ḥaʼil, and at its peak around the 1850s, the Shammar ruled much of central and northern Arabia from Riyadh to the frontiers of Syria and Upper Mesopotamia.

Originally residing in Yemen, the Hijaz between Mecca and Medina, they spread throughout the Arabian Peninsula and the Maghreb. They are known for their frequent travels, emigration and instability. When their population grew and their area of residence in Yemen, Najd and Hijaz became too narrow, they gathered to decide their future. They rejected conquering neighboring tribes (many being from Harb) and instead chose to migrate. They established the Emirate of Mahamid in the Karak region after moving through Dumat Al-Jandal and the Levant, where they became one of the largest tribes and allied with local clans like Al-Omaro Bani Uqba.

name: Manasir
legitimacyNotes: Critical research suggests that the current Manasir community should be viewed as a voluntary amalgamation throughout the centuries between indigenous mostly Nubian groups, descendants of emigrating Arabs and recruited tribal minorities living among them or in the adjoining areas. By recognizing a common genealogical pedigree, all members establish a system of mutual respect, rights and obligations.
ancestorStory: According to the current oral tradition of many Manasir, their ancestor is called Mansur and belongs to the line of descendants of al-'Abbas, the uncle of Muhammad. Multiple explanations are offered: some claim their apical forefather is Mansur bin Qahtan belonging to the Southern Arabs called Qahtaniin; others trace their origin to the Kawahla saying they are sons of Mansur bin 'Aim; still others insist they are descendants of cousins from a Shaiqi clan called al-Hankab who migrated after internal fights, with Mansur being a direct descendant of King Sabir.
traditionalEconomy: The riverain Manasir pursue small scale agriculture on alluvial soils in the immediate vicinity of the Nile. Their most important cash crop is a wide variety of dates. They are also renowned for their skill in building mud houses and used to float wood from the region of Atbarah. The Bedouin portion herds goats, sheep and camels in desert valleys.
description: The Manasir constitute one of many Sunni Arab riverine tribes of Northern Sudan. They inhabit the region of the Fourth Cataract of the Nile and call their homeland Dar al-Manasir. Similar to their neighbouring tribes, the upstream Rubatab and the downstream Shaiqiyah, the Manasir are an indigenous Nile culture who adopted Islam and speak Arabic as their first language. Unlike other riverine tribes of Sudan, a considerable part of their population lives as Bedouins in the adjacent Bayuda Desert. The nomadic life of herding their stock of goats, sheep and camels in desert valleys is however limited for many to the rainy season, coinciding with the annual inundation of the Nile.

description: The Mazari represent multiple distinct tribal groups rather than a single unified tribe. The text identifies at least two separate Mazari tribes: an Emirati Bedouin tribe of the United Arab Emirates and a Baloch tribe settled in Rajanpur and Sindh, Pakistan. These appear to be separate entities that share only the name 'Mazari', likely derived from different etymological origins.

name: Mazrui
description: The Mazrui or Mazar'i were an Omani Arab clan that reigned over some areas of East Africa, especially Kenya, from the 18th to the 20th century. In the 18th century they governed Mombasa and other coastal places and opposed the Omani Al Bu Sa'id Dynasty that ruled over Zanzibar. They were one of the groups that most actively resisted British rule when the British East Africa Protectorate was established in the late 19th century, along with the Kikuyu and Kamba people.

relations.context: Opposed the Omani Al Bu Sa'id Dynasty that ruled over Zanzibar in the 18th century

name: Mudar
legitimacyNotes: Modern scholarship treats these genealogies with caution, noting that they reflect later attempts to systematize tribal relations and political rivalries rather than straightforward history. The confederation included diverse tribes with varying lifestyles and political orientations grouped together under the collective name 'Mudar'.
ancestorName: Mudar b. Nizār b. Maʿadd b. ʿAdnān
ancestorStory: Genealogists trace Mudar's descent from Mudar b. Nizār b. Maʿadd b. ʿAdnān, placing him among the foundational ancestors of the so-called 'northern Arabs'. Mudar is said to have had two sons, from whom the major branches of the confederation descended. Genealogical narratives also preserve legendary elements, such as the division of Nizār's inheritance and symbolic associations.
traditionalEconomy: Mixed - ranging from sedentary groups associated with sanctuary towns to highly mobile pastoral nomads
description: Mudar was one of the principal tribal confederations of northern and central Arabia in late antiquity and the early Islamic period. Rising to power over the Hejaz in the fifth century after supplanting the Banu Ghassan, Mudar occupied large areas of the Hejaz, Tihamah, Najd, and adjoining regions. Together with Rabīʿa, it formed one of the two largest groupings of tribes tracing descent from Nizar ibn Maʿadd. In later historiography, 'Mudar' came to designate not only a genealogical lineage but also a broad political and cultural bloc opposed to the southern (Yemeni) tribal groupings.

subTribes.name: Quraysh
subTribes.legitimacyNotes: True blood lineage traced to Fihr ibn Malik of the Kinana tribe, with clear genealogical connections to Adnan. The tribal name either derives from 'taqarrush' meaning 'coming together' or from their ancestor Qusayy ibn Kilab who unified the scattered descendants.
subTribes.ancestorStory: Fihr ibn Malik was the progenitor of the Quraysh, descended from Adnan the Ishmaelite. He led the warriors of Kinana and Khuzayma in defense of the Kaaba against tribes from Yemen. His descendants lived in scattered, nomadic groups among their Kinana relatives until Qusayy ibn Kilab, a sixth-generation descendant, unified them and established Quraysh control over Mecca.
subTribes.traditionalEconomy: Trade caravans, pilgrimage services, mining, taxes from pilgrims
subTribes.description: The Quraysh are an Arab tribe who controlled Mecca before the rise of Islam and became wealthy merchants dominating trade between the Indian Ocean, East Africa, and the Mediterranean. They were divided into ten main clans including the Banu Hashim, into which Prophet Muhammad was born. Initially opposed to Islam, they eventually converted after Muhammad's conquest of Mecca in 630 CE. After Muhammad's death, leadership of the Muslim community traditionally passed to members of the Quraysh.

name: Mutayr
legitimacyNotes: The tribal lineage appears to be authentic blood lineage supported by DNA testing showing shared haplogroups and common ancestry among members. The tribe has embraced genetic genealogy to study their genealogy using modern technologies.
ancestorStory: According to Ahmad al-Qalqashandi (d. 1418), the Mutayr tribe belongs to Ghatafan who are descendants of Ishmael son of Abraham. DNA tests have confirmed that male participants from Mutayr share the same haplogroup and common ancestor, with most Y-Chromosome lineage from the J1 Haplogroup network.
description: Mutayr is a large Arab tribe originating from the northern Hejaz near Medina, whose territory extends from the Hijaz to Najd and then to Kuwait. The tribe underwent a major eastward migration in the 17th century into central Arabia, becoming the dominant nomadic tribe of Al-Qaseem. They played a crucial role in early 20th century Arabian history through their involvement in the Ikhwan movement under Faisal Al-Dewish. Originally Bedouin, the tribe has now largely settled in cities and towns across Saudi Arabia, establishing up to 400 villages across the country.

name: Na'im
legitimacyNotes: Established blood lineage tribe with historical territorial control and recognized sub-tribal structure
description: The Na'im is a traditionally Ghafiri Arab tribe prominent in the United Arab Emirates and other Gulf countries. Historically one of the most significant tribes in the hinterland, they were widely distributed across Trucial Oman and the Sultanate of Oman. At their peak in the early 19th century, they numbered around 20,000 men in Buraimi and were arguably the dominant force west of the Hajar Mountains. The tribe's traditional heartland was the Buraimi oasis and nearby Al Ain, where they expanded at the expense of the Dhawahir tribe. Despite their historical prominence, the tribe experienced decline due to persistent internal divisions among sub-tribes, with their fighting strength dropping from 2,000 men in the early 20th century to just 300-400 rifles by the 1940s.

subTribes.name: Khawatir
subTribes.legitimacyNotes: The Khawatir are described as a legitimate subsection of the Na'im tribe, indicating a genuine blood lineage relationship rather than a political confederation.
subTribes.ancestorStory: <UNKNOWN>
subTribes.traditionalEconomy: Bedouin pastoralism with camels, sheep, goats, cattle, and date palm cultivation
subTribes.description: The Khawatir is an Arab tribe of the United Arab Emirates, forming one of three subsections of the Na'im tribe. A Bedouin tribe whose traditional territory stretched from Buraimi north to the Jiri Plain, they mostly settled around the Buraimi oasis. They adopted Wahhabism in the 19th century in response to encroachment by the Al Bu Falah and their Manasir allies. The tribe frequently came into conflict with the Sharqiyin of Fujairah but would ally with them against the powerful Qawasim of Sharjah and Ras Al Khaimah. They also settled in Dhaid in Sharjah and were involved in various political disputes and alliances throughout their history.

subTribes.name: Al Bu Shamis
subTribes.legitimacyNotes: Originally one of three sections of the larger Na'im tribal confederation, the Al Bu Shamis evolved from being part of a larger tribal structure to becoming virtually independent, particularly through close association with the Al Bu Falah ruling family of Abu Dhabi
subTribes.ancestorStory: <UNKNOWN>
subTribes.traditionalEconomy: Bedouin nomadism, later settled agriculture in oases and strategic coastal settlements
subTribes.description: The Al Bu Shamis is an Arab tribe of the United Arab Emirates, also evident in northern Oman. Originally one of three sections of the Na'im confederation (along with Al Bu Kharaiban and Khawatir), the Al Bu Shamis has become virtually independent and closely associated with the ruling Al Bu Falah of Abu Dhabi. Originally forming the Bedouin component of the Na'im, they retained a predominantly nomadic character into the 1950s with approximately 1,000 individuals including around 400 armed men. They later settled strategically significant oases and coastal settlements, becoming headmen in places like Hamriyah and Al Heera.

name: Naqbiyin
legitimacyNotes: Appears to be a genuine tribal lineage with clear territorial associations and historical presence in the region dating back at least 300 years in some locations.
description: The Naqbiyin is a tribe of the United Arab Emirates that has been influential in the tribal politics of Sharjah and Ras Al Khaimah emirates. By the early 20th century, they had settled throughout the region including Khor Fakkan, Kalba, Dibba, and Fahlain, with a population of approximately 1,800 people primarily engaged in agriculture. They are known for their strategic settlements and close relationship with the Qawasim rulers.

name: Otaibah
legitimacyNotes: The tribe claims descent from pre-Islamic Hawazin through documented genealogical records. While most genealogy is based on oral tradition dating to the Middle Ages and earlier, there is scholarly verification through historical manuscripts including Hisham ibn al-Kalbi's 8th-century work 'The Great Ancestry'.
ancestorName: Otaibah ibn Guzayah ibn Jusham ibn Sa'd ibn Bakr ibn Hawazin
ancestorStory: According to the 8th-century manuscript by Hisham ibn al-Kalbi, 'Otaibah' is attributed to a standard or banner belonging to the tribe of Hawazin. The name derives from Otaibah Ibn Guzayah Ibn Jusham Ibn Sa'd ibn Bakr Ibn Hawazin. After most Hawazin clans departed from Hejaz and Najd, those who remained formed the largest Hawazin alliance and were named Otaibah.
description: The Otaibah is one of the biggest Arabian tribes originating in the Arabian Peninsula, with distribution spanning throughout Saudi Arabia, especially in Najd and Hejaz, and the Middle East. They are descended from the Bedouin and trace back to the Mudar family, belonging to the Qays ʿAylān confederacy through its previous name, Hawazin. The tribe remained as the largest Hawazin alliance after most other Hawazin clans departed from Hejaz and Najd to other lands including the Levant, Iraq, Egypt, North Africa, and Persia.

subTribes.name: Himyar
subTribes.legitimacyNotes: Originally a confederation of tribes that formed a unified kingdom; ruled by kings from the dhū-Raydān tribe after independence from Qataban
subTribes.traditionalEconomy: international trade in frankincense, myrrh, and ivory; hub linking Mediterranean, Middle East, India, and East Africa
subTribes.description: Himyar was a powerful ancient kingdom and polity in the southern highlands of Yemen that existed from 110 BCE until the early 6th century CE. Initially integrated into the Qatabanian kingdom until 110 BCE, it became an independent kingdom ruled by kings from the dhū-Raydān tribe. The kingdom was a confederation of tribes that created a wealthy, sophisticated society with rich trade networks linking the Mediterranean, Middle East, India, and East Africa. Himyar conquered neighboring kingdoms including Saba', Qataban, and Haḍramaut, uniting southern Arabia for the first time by 300 CE. The kingdom underwent significant religious transformations, converting from South Arabian polytheism to Judaism in the 4th century under Malkikarib Yuhamin, and later to Christianity after the Aksumite conquest in 530 CE.

subTribes.name: Kahlan
subTribes.legitimacyNotes: A major tribal confederation of ancient Saba', with genuine historical presence in Yemen before forced migration due to Himyarite pressure
subTribes.ancestorName: Kahlan bin Saba bin Yishjab bin Yarub bin Qahtan
subTribes.ancestorStory: Descended from Kahlan bin Saba bin Yishjab bin Yarub bin Qahtan, making them one of the main Qahtani confederations originating from ancient Saba' kingdom in Yemen.
subTribes.peakPowerEra: Ancient Sabaean period, before 2nd century BC
subTribes.traditionalEconomy: Highland pastoralism and agriculture in Yemen, later nomadic pastoralism during migrations
subTribes.description: Kahlan was one of the main tribal confederations of Saba' in Ancient Yemen, descended from Kahlan bin Saba bin Yishjab bin Yarub bin Qahtan. Originally dominant in the Yemeni highlands, they were forced out by Himyar in the 2nd century BC and migrated northwards throughout Arabia after the destruction of the Marib Dam in the 3rd century AD, eventually reaching as far as Mesopotamia and Syria.

subTribes.name: Qahtan (tribe)
subTribes.legitimacyNotes: This is a major tribal confederation with documented historical presence across the Arabian Peninsula. The confederation structure with three main constituent tribes (Sanhan, Junb, Rufaida) suggests a political alliance rather than pure blood lineage.
subTribes.description: Qahtān is an Arab tribal confederation composed of three main tribes: Sanhan, Junb, and Rufaida. Today, members of the tribe and its sub-tribes are based across the Arabian Peninsula including Yemen, Saudi Arabia, Oman, Qatar, Iraq, Kuwait, and the United Arab Emirates. The tribe enjoyed prominence in the Najd region, particularly in Al-Quwai'iyah, and expanded its influence in southern Najd before later returning to its original homeland in the south.

subTribes.ancestorStory: Al Hawajer are a Qahtanite tribe from Abida, they resided near Sarat Abida also known as 'alhirja' near Thahran Al Janoub
subTribes.description: Bani Hajer, also known as al-Hawajir, are an influential Qahtanite tribe in the Arabian Peninsula. Originally from Abida near Sarat Abida (also known as 'alhirja' near Thahran Al Janoub), they are known for their trade activities and bringing foreign goods to Arabia. They migrated to Lower Najd in the early 13th century under Sheikh Mohammed Bin Shaba'an, then later to Al Ahsa'a. They are considered nomadic Bedouins, though many have settled in cities and hold government offices. They were honored by King Abdulaziz Al Saud with the title 'Khaznat Thafar' (Vault of Victory) for their crucial role in the Battle of Kinzan against Al Ajman in 1914-1915.

The Al Qubaisi family shares close relations with the leading royal family of the United Arab Emirates through Salama bint Butti Al Qubaisi, mother of Sheikh Zayed bin Sultan Al Nahyan, the founder of the UAE. As a subset of the Bani Yas tribe, the Al Qubaisi family has been influential in various aspects of Emirati society, including politics, business, and community affairs. They have played roles in the development and progress of the UAE, contributing to various sectors and institutions within the country.

name: Rabi'ah
legitimacyNotes: Established blood lineage recognized by classical Arab genealogists as one of the two main Adnanite branches
ancestorStory: Rabi'a ibn Nizar is considered the patriarch of one of the two main branches of the North Arabian (Adnanite) tribes, representing a major division in Arabian tribal genealogy.

description: Rabi'a ibn Nizar is the patriarch of one of two main branches of the 'North Arabian' (Adnanite) tribes, the other being founded by Mudhar. The tribe has numerous important branches including Abd al-Qays, Anizah, Banu Bakr, Taghlib, and others. Originally from the Tihamah region of western Arabia, they migrated northwards and eastwards, with different branches settling across the Arabian Peninsula, Mesopotamia, and eventually Egypt. Many were Christian before Islam, and the tribe produced numerous notable figures throughout Islamic history.

subTribes.name: Banu Hanifa
subTribes.legitimacyNotes: Classical Arab genealogists counted them as a Christian branch of Bani Bakr, but they led an independent existence prior to Islam. Later assimilated into Anizah tribe.
subTribes.description: Banu Hanifa is an ancient Arab tribe that inhabited the area of al-Yamama in central Saudi Arabia. Members of the great Rabi'ah branch of North Arabian tribes, they were primarily sedentary farmers living in settlements along the wadis of eastern Nejd. The tribe played a significant role in early Islamic history, initially converting to Islam but then apostatizing under their own prophet Musaylima, leading to the Ridda Wars. After their defeat, they rejoined the Islamic state and later spread to various regions including Iraq and Egypt. The ruling House of Saud of Saudi Arabia are Hanifites, and according to King Salman Al Saud, the tribe eventually assimilated into the larger Anizah tribe.

It is the largest branch of the Tayy and one of the largest and most influential Arab tribes. The historical seat of tribal leadership is in Ḥaʼil, and at its peak around the 1850s, the Shammar ruled much of central and northern Arabia from Riyadh to the frontiers of Syria and Upper Mesopotamia.

description: The Sharqiyin were long the dominant (traditionally Ghafiri) tribe along the East coast of the Trucial States and the second most numerous in the area around the start of the 19th century. They settled all along the East Coast from Kalba to Dibba, as well as in the Wadi Ham and Jiri plain. By the turn of the 20th century they were some 7,000 strong. After the Bani Yas, the Sharqiyin were the second most numerous tribe in the Trucial States. They were originally dependents of Sharjah but made several attempts to secede and declare independence, finally practically managing this from 1901 onwards, gaining British recognition as a Trucial State, Fujairah, in 1952.

description: The Shihuh is an Arab tribe living in the United Arab Emirates (UAE) and Oman, inhabiting the northern part of the Hajar Mountain range, specifically in the Ruus Al Jibal (Musandam Peninsula). The tribe has long been influential in the affairs of both the east and west coast settlements of the northern UAE and Oman and has fiercely maintained both its identity and independence. They are essentially agricultural during winter months in mountain communities, migrating to coastal areas during warmer months for date season work and pearl fishing.

subTribes.legitimacyNotes: Appears to be a genuine tribal section of the larger Shihuh confederation
subTribes.traditionalEconomy: Pearling and farming - seasonal work in pearling industry and agriculture in mountain regions
subTribes.description: The Bani Hadiyah is a tribe of the United Arab Emirates, originating in Ras Al Khaimah and also in areas of Northern Oman. They are one of the two main sections of the Shihuh tribe, along with the Bani Shatair. The tribe is divided into sections including the Kumzarah and Habus. Traditionally, they practiced seasonal migration, working in Ras Al Khaimah during the pearling season and farming in the Ruus Al Jibal area of the Hajar Mountains during winter.

subTribes.name: Bani Shatair
subTribes.traditionalEconomy: Pearling and farming - worked in Ras Al Khaimah and Rams during pearling season, farming in the Ruus Al Jibal area of the Hajar Mountains during winter
subTribes.description: Bani Shatair is a tribal name originating in Ras Al Khaimah, UAE and areas of Northern Oman. They are one of two main sections of the Shihuh tribe, traditionally engaged in seasonal migration between coastal pearling work and mountain farming. The village of Sha'am was originally settled entirely by the Bani Shatair.

name: Subay'
legitimacyNotes: Established blood lineage tracing to the ancient and powerful Banu 'Amir tribe that dominated central Arabia in medieval times
ancestorStory: The family traces its lineage to the large, ancient tribe of Banu 'Amir, also known as 'Amir ibn Sa'sa'a, who came to dominate central Arabia in the medieval period.
description: Subaie' is an Arabian tribe living in the center of southern Najd. The tribe comprises both nomadic (bedouin) and sedentary sections, with the sedentary members having resided in central Arabia for centuries. Families belonging to Subay' can be found in practically every town in the region, making up a disproportionate amount of central Arabia's traditional sedentary population. They were known as Prophet Mohammed's knights and have served as knights for the first and second Saudi states as well as the current Kingdom of Saudi Arabia.

name: Sudan

Sudan is a country with a population of 51.8 million people as of 2025, occupying 1,886,068 square kilometres. The name Sudan derives from the Arabic bilād as-sūdān (بلاد السودان), meaning 'The Land of the Blacks', referring to the dark skin of the indigenous people. The text covers the country's history from prehistoric times through various kingdoms and colonial periods to modern independence.

name: Tayy
legitimacyNotes: Real blood lineage traced to Qahtanite origins, though Ibn Khaldun noted that Tayy intermarried with non-Arabs and did not preserve lineage purity strictly
ancestorName: Julhumah ibn Udad (known as Tayy or Tayyi)
ancestorStory: According to early Arab genealogists, Julhumah ibn Udad was the progenitor of the Tayy. His surname 'Ṭayyiʾ' supposedly derived from the Arabic word 'ṭawā' meaning 'to plaster' because he was said to be the first to have plastered the walls of a well. His ancestry was traced to Kahlan ibn Saba ibn Ya'rub, great-grandson of Qahtan.
traditionalEconomy: pastoralism and control of trade routes
description: The Tayy are a large and ancient Arab tribe, among whose descendants today are the tribes of Bani Sakher and Shammar. Originally based in Yemen, they migrated to northern Arabia in the late 2nd century CE and settled in the Shammar and Salma Mountains, which became known as Jabal Tayy. They established relations with both the Sasanian and Byzantine empires, supplanted the Lakhmids as rulers of al-Hirah in the 610s, and played significant roles throughout Islamic history. The tribe was split during the Fasad War, with the Jadila branch converting to Christianity and migrating to Syria, while the Ghawth branch remained in Jabal Tayy.

subTribes.name: Shammar
subTribes.legitimacyNotes: A genuine tribal confederation with three main branches (Abdah, Al-Aslam, and Sinjarah) that evolved from the larger Tayy tribal group. The name change from Tayy to Shammar occurred by the 14th century when they became known in literature as Shammar.
subTribes.ancestorStory: Descended from the Tayy tribe which migrated from Yemen to northern Arabian Peninsula. Led by Usma bin Luai, the Tayy invaded the mountains of Ajā and Salma from Banu Assad and Banu Tamim in northern Arabia in their exodus from Yemen in 115 CE.
subTribes.traditionalEconomy: nomadic camel-herding, horse-breeding, city-dwelling in Ha'il, and agriculture in oases
subTribes.description: The Shammar is a tribal Arab Qahtanite confederation descended from the Tayy, which migrated into northern Arabian Peninsula from Yemen in the second century. It is the largest branch of the Tayy and one of the largest and most influential Arab tribes. The historical seat of tribal leadership is in Ḥaʼil, and at its peak around the 1850s, the Shammar ruled much of central and northern Arabia from Riyadh to the frontiers of Syria and Upper Mesopotamia.

name: Tunaij
description: The Tunaij is an Arab tribe of the United Arab Emirates that primarily settled in the inland oasis town of Dhaid in Sharjah, the Ras Al Khaimah town of Rams, and small numbers at Hamriyah. At the turn of the 19th century, they numbered around 4,000 people in the Northern Emirates, with 1,500 being Bedouin. They were influential in tribal politics due to their ability to raise a force of 500 fighting men. The Bedouin Tunaij used Dhaid as their center with a fortified tower protecting their settlement. The Tunaij of Rams were primarily involved in pearl fishing, and during pearling season both Bedouin Tunaij and Shihuh would work as seasonal laborers on the coast.

name: Zaab
traditionalEconomy: pearling, date cultivation, fishing, and livestock (sheep and cattle)
description: The Za'ab is an Arab tribe of the Arabian Peninsula, principally in the United Arab Emirates. Originally settled the coastal village of Jazirat Al Hamra with around 500 houses at the turn of the 20th century, and also established themselves in Kalba with 150 families. The tribe was approximately 3,300 strong and maintained date palms in the inland village of Khatt. They operated a fleet of 25 pearling boats which provided their principal income until the pearl market crash in the late 1920s. Following disputes with Ras Al Khaimah rulers in 1968, the majority accepted Sheikh Zayed's offer to relocate to Abu Dhabi, leaving behind an almost completely abandoned village of 2,500 people.

name: Zahran
legitimacyNotes: This appears to be a genuine blood lineage tracing back to the ancient Azd confederation, with well-documented genealogical records and historical presence in the Arabian Peninsula.
ancestorName: Zahran ibn Ka'ab ibn Al-Harith ibn Ka'ab ibn Abdullah Ibn Mālik ibn Nasr ibn Al-Azd
ancestorStory: Zahran is the name of the shared common ancestor of the tribe. Etymological sources indicate it is of Arabic Semitic origin, meaning 'bright' and 'pure'. The full lineage traces back to Al-Azd through Zahran ibn Ka'ab.
description: Zahran (Arabic: زهران), also known as Banū ʿZahrān ibn Kaʿab, is one of the oldest Arabian tribes in the Arabian Peninsula and is regarded as one of the largest tribes in Al Bahah Province. Al Baha is the homeland of Zahran and Ghamid. The tribe is well-known from pre-Islamic times and many joined Prophet Muhammad in Medina during the early Islamic period. The name Zahran means 'bright' and 'pure' in Arabic.
